articles.title: Winners revealed: Australian Urban Design Awards 2026
articles.summary: The 2026 Australian Urban Design Awards have named twelve winners — nine projects drawn from a 27-strong shortlist plus three individuals — across four categories: Built Outcomes, Research and Advocacy, Strategic Design and Policy, and Urban Design Champions. Over 80 entries came in this cycle. The Built Outcomes category produced three joint winners that read, together, as a program statement about what Australian urban design is prizing in 2026. Balam Balam Place in Brunswick, Melbourne, by Kennedy Nolan with Open Work and Finding Infinity, was commended as a socially inclusive civic precinct that renews existing heritage fabric rather than replacing it. The St Kilda Pier Redevelopment by Jackson Clements Burrows Architects, Site Office Landscape Architecture and AW Maritime was described by the jury as a major piece of civic engineering that is both resilient and whimsical, and a vital component of enhanced public realm and consolidated coastal infrastructure responding to decades of master planning along the foreshore. The third co-winner, Hill Thalis Architecture and Urban Projects' Campbelltown Station Commuter Carpark, reconceives a utilitarian typology as civic infrastructure — not simply an infrastructure for cars, but a resilient, civic-minded and genuinely optimistic car park, in the practice's own framing. The jury — Matt Pullinger, Bill Tsakalos, Leigh Woolley and Emily Wong — read the wider field as a gentler, Indigenous- and community-led register: ecological landscapes, heritage continuity, and everyday gathering rather than signature architecture. Two Melbourne projects in the top tier signal the city's growing capacity to treat piers, laneways and heritage precincts as civic infrastructure in their own right.
articles.whyItMatters: Two of three Built Outcomes winners sit in Melbourne — a useful brief for how juries now read heritage reuse and waterfront civic engineering.

articles.title: Coldefy Leads Winning Masterplan to Transform Budapest Brownfield into Rewilded Urban District
articles.summary: A team led by French practice Coldefy — with CITYFÖRSTER, Sporaarchitects, TREIBHAUS.LAND and Marko & Placemakers — has won Budapest's largest urban competition, the Rákosrendező Masterplan, to redevelop a long-neglected railway brownfield on the eastern side of the Hungarian capital. The 15-year scheme targets over 10,000 apartments plus commercial, civic and cultural uses, distributed across six sub-districts linked by a continuous loop of public space, and structured around 15-minute city principles so that daily needs sit within walking distance of home. Landscape is the masterplan's backbone: a 15-hectare Forest Park connects across the existing rail line to a new Railway Park, with meadows, a central lake, event spaces, and an interwoven blue-and-green infrastructure network designed to carry stormwater, cool summer temperatures and seed biodiversity back into what the city has long called its rust belt. The existing Rákosrendező station is reimagined as a multimodal hub anchoring the central urban district and plugging into an extended metro line, a new tram line and a network of active mobility routes, which in turn supports largely car-free residential streets and squares. Former railway depots and buildings are slated for adaptive reuse as a cultural centre housing a municipal library and a railway museum. The competition jury singled out the proposal for a livable residential environment and the exceptional quality of its public space system. Ecological continuity, not density alone, reads as the winning move, and the site's rail heritage is treated as civic patrimony rather than an obstacle to remediation.
articles.whyItMatters: A rare large-scale European masterplan where rewilded landscape, not the block plan, is the structuring idea — a precedent worth filing for Fishermans Bend.

articles.title: Maryland unveils plan for 5,000 homes near transit
articles.summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore has unveiled the Baltimore Regional Transit-Oriented Development Strategy, a state-led plan to convert 134 acres of state-owned land — most of it surface parking at or beside transit stations — into roughly 5,000 new homes, and to generate an estimated US$1.4 billion in combined state and local tax revenue. Of the 134 acres, 46 acres sit in Baltimore City, 66 in Baltimore County and 23 in Anne Arundel County, giving the strategy a regional footprint rather than a city-only one. The document is framed as a joint-development playbook for state agencies, local governments and private partners, with station-by-station briefs covering market readiness, zoning and available land, plus financing tools including tax increment financing, enterprise and opportunity-zone incentives, and a strategic loan fund administered by Maryland's economic development corporation. The first test site is the Rogers Avenue Metro Station, where the state is already seeking a development partner for roughly nine acres that could support up to 400 homes and generate about US$27 million in tax revenue. Moore paired the land strategy with a transit-service commitment — 78 new rail cars by 2027 — signalling that rebuilding ridership and rebuilding demand for station-adjacent housing are being treated as a single policy. For urban designers the strategy is notable less for its unit targets than for the land it identifies: parking lots at existing metro, light rail and MARC stations, framed not as infrastructure but as an underused land bank. It is a state-level answer to the familiar American problem that TOD parcels usually sit in municipal hands with mismatched zoning.
articles.whyItMatters: State-owned station parking reframed as a housing land bank — a play Victoria could run at PTV car parks alongside the Suburban Rail Loop.

articles.title: New 10-year plan for Perth city unveiled
articles.summary: The City of Perth has endorsed Shaping Perth City Centre – Towards 2036 and Beyond, a ten-year strategic framework developed with Danish urban strategy firm Gehl Architects, extending what is now a 30-year collaboration between the two. The plan sets five strategic goals and 20 implementation directions and identifies five catalytic Key Moves intended to act as the spine of the next decade of investment: North-South Connectors, the Buneenboro Loop, Living Streets, The Malls Evolution, and East-West Transit Corridors. The Buneenboro Loop, named with the Noongar term for the Swan River, is a continuous waterfront walking and cycling route stitching Derbarl Yerrigan, Kaarta Gar-up (Kings Park), the Perth Convention and Exhibition Centre, Elizabeth Quay and Langley Park into a single legible public realm. William Street is earmarked to become a living street – a green, walkable, bike-friendly spine with reduced through-traffic, dining edges and activated ground floors – while Barrack Street is reframed as a cultural axis. A new People's Walk would connect Central Perth Station to Forrest Place, Carillon City and Elizabeth Quay, knitting the station forecourt to the river. The framework was developed with more than 80 stakeholders from private industry and government, and is pegged to a demographic target of 55,000 residents in the city centre by 2036 and up to 90,000 by 2050 – roughly doubling to tripling the current population. The Malls Evolution direction revisits Murray and Hay Street Malls as primary retail anchors needing curated programming, evening activation and a resilient tenancy mix to survive shifts in office attendance and online retail.
articles.whyItMatters: Indigenous-named waterfront loop plus a William Street 'living street' is a template Melbourne could port onto Elizabeth Street or the Yarra edge.

articles.title: NYC announces new 'pied-a-terre tax' on second homes
articles.summary: Mayor Zohran Mamdani and Governor Kathy Hochul announced on 15 April a joint proposal for New York's first pied-a-terre tax, an annual surcharge on one-to-three family homes, condominiums and co-ops valued above US$5 million when the owner's primary residence sits outside New York City. The city estimates roughly 13,000 properties would be affected and projects the tax would raise at least US$500 million a year – revenue the mayor argues will help close the city's spending deficit and fund housing, transit and public-service priorities. Polling cited by the mayor's office put support among New Yorkers at 93 percent, while the real estate lobby has pushed back hard, warning the surcharge would depress property values, deter investment and cut construction jobs. The measure still needs to pass the New York State Legislature, where similar proposals have stalled before. For urban designers, the proposal matters less as a tax story than as a signal about the city's posture toward housing that sits empty most of the year. Shadow-city anxieties – lights-off condo towers along 57th Street's Billionaires' Row – have shaped debate for over a decade, but this is the first time a sitting mayor and governor have formally aligned behind pricing the external cost of that absenteeism. The policy does not, by itself, return those units to resident use, but it reframes ultra-luxury second homes as a drain on the tax base rather than a civic trophy, and makes land-value capture at the top of the market politically live again.
articles.whyItMatters: Live test of whether taxing vacant luxury housing nudges supply – a question Melbourne also faces with its vacant residential land tax and absentee-owner surcharge.

articles.title: Reclaiming the Street: Alejandra Ferrera on Architecture and Urban Life in Honduras
articles.summary: Honduran architect Alejandra Ferrera argues in a long ArchDaily interview that decades of security-driven design have hollowed out the public realm in Tegucigalpa and other Honduran cities, reducing the street to a transit void rather than a place of social encounter. Raised in Danli and trained across Brazil, the Netherlands and Australia, Ferrera brings fifteen years of comparative practice to the diagnosis. The shift she traces is familiar across much of Latin America: high perimeter walls, gated compounds, privatised amenities, and a retreat of middle-class domestic life from the sidewalk inward. Ferrera accepts that fear was a reasonable response to real crime in the 1990s and 2000s, but argues the city never rebuilt the connective tissue that walls replaced – porous ground floors, shaded seating, legible public frontages, small commerce along the kerb. The consequence, she argues, is a wounded national identity that instinctively looks to Miami or Madrid for design references and dismisses the ordinary fabric of Honduran barrios as backdrop rather than raw material. Her own practice sits in explicit opposition: projects that re-privilege the street, reuse local timber and masonry, and treat vernacular typologies as design intelligence rather than nostalgia. Ferrera points to heritage-led work in Comayagua and incremental public-space repairs in Tegucigalpa as evidence the pattern can be reversed, and calls on younger Honduran practices to stop treating security and good public space as a tradeoff. The piece lands as several Latin American cities openly reposition public safety as an urban-design outcome rather than a security-forces one.
articles.whyItMatters: Hard counter to defensive design: reminds designers that active frontage and eyes-on-the-street are a safety strategy, not just amenity.

articles.title: 25-year masterplan unveiled to transform Sydney Olympic Park
articles.summary: The New South Wales government has finalised the Sydney Olympic Park Master Plan 2050, a 25-year blueprint to convert the 640-hectare former Games precinct into a mixed-use urban neighbourhood for 15,000 new homes alongside its existing sporting and entertainment functions. Launched on 1 April 2026, the plan was designed by SJB with landscape architects Turf Design Studio and Indigenous design practice Yerrabingin, supported by SGS Economic and Planning, Cred Consulting, Atelier 10 and WSP. It builds on a draft exhibited in October 2024, with the finalised document adding 2,000 homes beyond the draft's figure. Up to 20 percent of dwellings on government-owned land and 5 percent on private land will be affordable. The masterplan protects and enhances 430 hectares of public open space and layers in 10 new playgrounds, nine new outdoor public spaces and four new sports fields. A new innovation, culture and commercial precinct will sit alongside a cultural centre, a library and community hub, and a sports and leisure centre, pivoting the park from event-day monoculture toward weekday civic life. Tree canopy and biodiversity across the existing wetlands and parklands will be expanded. The plan is pegged to major transport investments including Sydney Metro West and the Parramatta Light Rail extension, with dense pedestrian and cycling networks woven through the precinct. The Homebush Bay Drive roundabout upgrade, jointly state and federally funded, underpins the road network. Together, the moves reposition Olympic Park from a special-purpose venue to a transit-oriented, mixed-use district anchored by stadiums and green infrastructure.
articles.whyItMatters: A precedent for retrofitting event-dominated, special-purpose precincts into transit-oriented, mixed-use neighbourhoods with affordable housing baked in.

articles.title: Coldefy leads winning masterplan to transform Budapest brownfield into rewilded urban district
articles.summary: French architecture practice Coldefy, leading a team that includes CITYFÖRSTER, Sporaarchitects, TREIBHAUS.LAND and Marko & Placemakers, has won the competition to design a masterplan for Rákosrendező, a long-derelict railway-yard site on Budapest's eastern edge that the city has called its rust belt. The 15-year scheme stitches over 10,000 apartments, commercial and civic programme, and a new multimodal transport hub onto the contaminated brownfield, with the existing Rákosrendező station reimagined as the heart of the precinct and linked to an extended metro line, a new tram line and a network of active mobility routes. Six sub-districts, each with its own character, are threaded together by a continuous loop of public space aligned with 15-minute city principles. The landscape strategy treats ecology as primary infrastructure: a 15-hectare Forest Park anchors the site, connected via linear parks to a new Railway Park that incorporates meadows, event spaces and a central lake. The Rákos Stream, previously channelised, is renaturalised as a meandering waterway with accessible terraces, paths and rest areas along its reshaped banks. Blue and green systems are explicitly combined to form a continuous ecological corridor through the district. The jury praised the scheme for a livable residential environment and the exceptional quality of its public space system. The design team positions rewilding as a generator of public life rather than a decorative overlay, with parks structured to activate the six new neighbourhoods. The project offers a European precedent for converting obsolete rail infrastructure into transit-oriented, ecologically driven urban fabric at scale.
articles.whyItMatters: A template for turning contaminated rail yards into 15-minute, transit-anchored districts where rewilded landscape does the structural work, not decoration.

articles.title: Philadelphia gained 4,000 new housing permits per year after upzoning
articles.summary: A new Urban Institute study analysed 15 years of zoning changes in New York City and Philadelphia and found that broad upzoning reforms measurably increased housing supply, though with substantial variation across neighbourhoods. In Philadelphia, citywide upzoning helped generate as many as 4,000 additional housing unit permits per year compared with non-upzoned parcels, with the strongest effect appearing between 2019 and 2021. In New York, seven neighbourhood-scale upzonings collectively produced more than 4,000 additional housing units within four years beyond the counterfactual. Reporting on the study for Planetizen, Diana Ionescu highlights the finding that both cities saw significant supply gains from rezoning, but not uniformly. Upzoned parcels in areas with pre-existing weaker housing markets saw little uplift in permitting or completions; the neighbourhoods that captured the bulk of new development were already attracting investment before the rezoning. The researchers argue this confirms zoning reform is necessary but insufficient on its own, and that permits do not automatically translate into built units. The policy takeaway is that upzoning needs to be paired with complementary interventions such as infrastructure, subsidy, demand-side support and with direct affordability and anti-displacement measures, particularly in hot-market areas where supply gains risk concentrating where they are least needed for relative affordability. The study adds empirical weight to the emerging consensus that the supply-alone narrative overstates what zoning reform can deliver, while also confirming that upzoning does move the needle on permits at a scale large enough to matter for citywide housing policy.
articles.whyItMatters: Upzoning produces permits but not uniformly — pair it with investment and anti-displacement levers, or the supply gains cluster in already-hot submarkets.

articles.title: Winners revealed: Australian Urban Design Awards 2026
articles.summary: The 2026 Australian Urban Design Awards were unveiled in Canberra, recognising twelve winners across nine projects and three individuals selected from a 27-project shortlist. The Built Outcomes category — the core recognition for realised civic work — delivered three winners that collectively map the current range of Australian urban design practice. Melbourne's St Kilda Pier Redevelopment, delivered by Jackson Clements Burrows Architects with Site Office Landscape Architecture and AW Maritime, was described by the jury as a major piece of civic engineering that is both resilient and whimsical, acknowledging a project that rebuilt a climate-exposed bayside pier as a continuous piece of public infrastructure rather than a nostalgic restoration. Alongside it, Balam Balam Place in Brunswick by Kennedy Nolan with Open Work and Finding Infinity was recognised as a socially inclusive civic precinct developed through a sensitive approach to the renewal of existing heritage fabric — heritage-led urbanism treated as live infrastructure rather than museum practice. The third Built Outcomes winner, Campbelltown Station Commuter Carpark in Sydney by Hill Thalis Architecture and Urban Projects, reconceived a purely utilitarian building type as enduring civic infrastructure animated by colour and planting, signalling the jury's interest in dignifying the everyday pieces of the transit network rather than reserving design attention for marquee stations. Read together, the three winners suggest the awards are rewarding projects that reframe infrastructure — a pier, a heritage precinct, a park-and-ride — as long-life civic assets with visible landscape and social programming.
articles.whyItMatters: Three infrastructure types — pier, heritage precinct, commuter carpark — reframed as civic assets. Design discipline now applies to the everyday pieces too.

articles.title: Chicago eliminates parking requirements near transit
articles.summary: Chicago has fully eliminated minimum parking requirements for new housing within walking distance of Chicago Transit Authority rail stations, Metra stations and high-frequency bus corridors, completing a two-stage reform that now aligns the city with the incoming Illinois People Over Parking Act. The local ordinance builds on the 2022 Connected Communities Ordinance, which halved parking minimums in transit-served areas and allowed zero parking for assisted or affordable projects, and a 2025 amendment that let developments outside downtown provide zero parking without administrative adjustment. The latest change is the clean version: no minimum, no case-by-case ask. Paired with the reform, the city expanded by-right accessory dwelling units to all multifamily residential districts, meaning zoning now pulls in the same direction rather than creating a workaround. The state law, the People Over Parking Act, takes effect 1 June 2026 and prohibits any Illinois municipality from imposing minimum automobile parking on projects within half a mile of a transit hub or an eighth of a mile of a transit corridor, with hotels and motels carved out. Researchers cited in the coverage estimate surface parking spaces add more than 4,000 dollars per unit to construction costs and structured spaces more than 37,000 dollars, costs that have historically flowed through to rent — roughly a 17 percent premium in some studies. For practitioners, the reform consolidates a template: eliminate minimums, pair with ADU liberalisation, and let the housing rather than the car define block-scale form.
articles.whyItMatters: Illinois becomes the fourth US state to end transit-area parking minimums. The template — end minimums, pair with ADU liberalisation — is ready to adapt.

articles.title: Atlanta's Beltline hits 79% of its affordable housing goal
articles.summary: Atlanta Beltline, Inc. has announced it is at 79 percent of its 2030 affordable housing target, reporting 4,425 affordable or preserved units delivered inside the Beltline Tax Allocation District against a goal of 5,600 units. The progress reframes a project that was flagged in earlier years as falling behind: the agency delivered 299 units in 2025 and is projecting another 300 to 500 per year through the late 2020s, with a live pipeline of roughly 500 units currently in active development. Three projects carry the near-term weight. 579 Garson Drive, expected to close in the second quarter of 2026, will deliver 130 permanently affordable homes alongside 8,000 square feet of commercial space. 350 Chappell Road, a 6.3-acre site targeting 218 affordable units at 30 to 80 percent area median income, is scheduled to close this year. Waterworks opened in December 2025 with 100 studio apartments structured as rapid housing for formerly unhoused residents. The Beltline is also running a displacement prevention program that subsidises property-tax increases for long-time residents through 2030 — an explicit acknowledgement that the greenway has generated value uplift that falls on the people it was meant to serve. A new Affordable Housing Dashboard launched in January 2026 maps each delivered development and its affordability band, making the 4,425-unit figure independently auditable. For designers, the lesson is that a linear greenway functions as land policy: the trail programs the zone of uplift, and the housing target plus dashboard are how that uplift is captured.
articles.whyItMatters: The trail creates the uplift; the affordable housing target and dashboard decide who keeps it. A loop Melbourne's greenway corridors will eventually need.

articles.title: NSW government proposes single statewide community consultation framework
articles.summary: The NSW government has released a draft statewide Community Participation Plan that would replace more than 100 separate local plans with a single, consistent process for how the public is consulted on planning proposals across the state. The draft, released on 8 April by the Minns Labor government, addresses what the planning department describes as "inconsistent consultation practices and confusion" — a patchwork where timeframes, notification thresholds and exhibition requirements vary between councils, leaving residents, practitioners and applicants unsure how to engage. Under the draft, minimum consultation periods for significant state-level strategic planning initiatives extend from 45 to 60 days, reflecting an argument that strategic plans — the instruments that actually shape precincts and metropolitan structure — deserve more scrutiny, not less. Notification of complying development is standardised with a mandatory seven-day notice to neighbours before works begin. In a deliberate trade-off, councils will no longer need to publicly exhibit low-impact rules-compliant applications like houses on compliant lots, sheds and pools, speeding up assessment by removing exhibition overhead on applications where there is nothing meaningful to consult on. The draft preserves councils' ability to adapt their own engagement strategies to local circumstances, meaning place-specific processes can still sit over the top of the standardised base layer. Peak development bodies have welcomed the move as reducing the overhead of operating across multiple LGAs; local government associations have pushed back on aspects that narrow council discretion. The draft and accompanying discussion paper are on public exhibition until 3 June 2026 through the NSW Planning Portal, with submissions open to individuals, councils, industry bodies and community groups.
articles.whyItMatters: Consultation frameworks are urban design infrastructure. Who gets consulted on which strategic plans shapes what precincts become — a live precedent as Plan Melbourne's next refresh looms.

articles.title: Maryland unveils plan for 5,000 homes near Baltimore transit stations
articles.summary: Maryland Governor Wes Moore and Baltimore Mayor Brandon Scott have unveiled the Baltimore Regional Transit-Oriented Development Strategy, a plan to activate 134 acres of state-owned land near Baltimore metro and light rail stations for roughly 5,000 new homes. The sites — most currently operating as surface parking lots spread across 17 stations in Baltimore City and Anne Arundel and Baltimore counties — are explicitly targeted because they are already publicly owned, already adjacent to high-capacity transit, and already producing minimal public value as asphalt. The state projects about 1.4 billion dollars in added state and local tax revenue over the life of the program. The first site moving to market is roughly nine acres surrounding the Rogers Avenue Metro Station in northwest Baltimore, where the state is seeking a development partner to deliver up to 400 homes, projected to generate about 27 million dollars in state and city tax revenue from that parcel alone. Mayor Scott framed the parking-lot-to-housing swap explicitly in urban design terms: reducing the need for people to drive to the station weakens the case for large park-and-ride footprints and lets the station precinct be rebuilt as a walkable neighbourhood. Moore paired the announcement with broader transit investment, including 78 new rail cars scheduled by 2027, and flagged a Maryland Transit and Housing Opportunity Act that would unlock more than 300 acres of state-owned land adjacent to existing transit stations. For designers, the precedent is the combination of an enabling law, a pre-identified portfolio of state-owned land, and a concrete first pipeline project released simultaneously — a delivery package rather than aspirational policy.
articles.whyItMatters: Surface parking at transit stations is latent public land. Releasing it as a packaged pipeline, not site-by-site, is the delivery pattern Plan Melbourne's activity centres need.

articles.title: Elevated Infrastructure and Public Space: Reclaiming the Ground Below
articles.summary: ArchDaily's latest editorial essay examines how cities are reclaiming the ambiguous spaces beneath elevated infrastructure — highway flyovers, metro viaducts, rail bridges — as intentional public realm rather than as leftover shade. The piece's framing is structural: elevation reorganises the city vertically, letting speed, capital and visibility flow above while producing a shaded, fragmented second ground below that rarely receives the same design attention as the structures above. Where that second ground is neglected, it becomes the residue of infrastructure — unprogrammed, often hostile, and culturally coded as abandoned. Where it is designed with the rigour of a plaza or park, it becomes a continuous civic landscape. The article draws on case studies including the Bentway Staging Grounds in Toronto, where the undercroft of the Gardiner Expressway has been reworked through lighting, flexible ground treatments and seasonal programming into a programmable public stage; Miyashita Park in Tokyo, an early prototype of re-engineering elevated rail fabric as park-over-rail; and the Taichung Green Corridor in Taiwan, where a former railway alignment has been rebuilt as a continuous public spine integrating landscape, mobility and social infrastructure. The recurring design lesson the piece distils is that reclaiming under-infrastructure space demands continuity — of lighting, of ground plane, of program — rather than site-by-site intervention. It also argues these spaces are uniquely valuable as climate infrastructure: already shaded, already cool, already connected to transit. For dense cities running out of available ground for parks, the space under viaducts reads as the last accessible reserve of civic space, and the precedents are now mature enough to adapt rather than reinvent.
articles.whyItMatters: Melbourne's West Gate and CityLink edges are the local equivalent — underused civic reserve in plain sight. Continuity of ground plane is the design move to steal.

articles.title: Country-centred urban design scheme wins 2026 AA Prize for Unbuilt Work
articles.summary: Whadjuk Balardong Elder Robyn Collard and architect Simon Pendal have won the 2026 AA Prize for Unbuilt Work with "Walyalup/Fremantle — What If," a speculative scheme that reimagines Fremantle's planning code through a Country-centred lens. Rather than redesign individual sites, the project proposes structural changes to local planning controls: a network of "garden archipelagos" — connected green spaces that thread through the city by linking yards, schoolyards, sports grounds, golf courses, mature reserves and natural bush — paired with discreet building density bonuses concentrated at the edges of each lot. The combination is calibrated to bring back Country across the metropolitan grain without the broad-brush demolition that traditional urban renewal demands. The submission positions Fremantle as a test case for what Pendal calls "layering new uses without subtraction": existing fabric is preserved, gardens and small businesses are folded into the same blocks as new homes, and pedestrian and ecological connections are formalised through code rather than negotiated case by case. Density bonuses act as the regulatory carrot; ecological connectivity is the binding rule that organises growth. The 2026 jury — Ingrid Bakker (Hassell), Kirsteen Mackay (Office for Design and Architecture SA), Deo Prasad (UNSW), Stuart Vokes (Vokes and Peters), and chair Georgia Birks (Architecture Australia) — singled out the project for translating Indigenous-led thinking into operational planning instruments rather than rhetoric. For Australian designers used to seeing Country acknowledgement as a project add-on, the win signals a serious appetite for embedding it inside the zoning code itself.
articles.whyItMatters: Country-led planning is finally being operationalised as zoning code, not just cultural protocol — density bonuses become the carrot, ecological connectivity the rule.

articles.title: Reimagining the Complete Neighborhood through Urban Renaturing
articles.summary: ArchDaily contributor Olivia Poston profiles ReGreeneration, a transnational European consortium led by C40 Cities and supported by ARUP, Placemaking Europe, Climato Sfera and Inetum, that operates explicitly at the intersection of urban ecology, public health, infrastructure and neighborhood-scale design. The project is funded under Horizon Europe — the EU's research and innovation programme launched in 2021 with a 95 billion euro commitment — and unites nine cities, their governing bodies, and leading research institutions in a coordinated effort to rebuild European neighbourhoods around climate resilience. The article's premise is structural: rather than treating greening as urban beautification or stormwater infrastructure as an engineering problem, ReGreeneration argues that European cities must fundamentally change how they are built and maintained if they are to remain liveable under accelerating climate pressure. The "complete neighbourhood" concept — usually used in North American planning to describe walkable, mixed-use districts with daily needs in proximity — is here recast through an ecological lens, with renaturing (the deliberate restoration of soil, hydrology, canopy and biodiversity into built fabric) treated as a foundational service alongside transport and housing. The piece collects case studies from participating cities, sketching how neighbourhood-scale interventions are being prototyped: depaved courtyards, rerouted stormwater, microforest plantings, cooling corridors, building retrofits. For practitioners outside Europe, the project is a useful precedent for how a public funder can underwrite the slow, expensive work of structural ecological retrofit at a scale individual councils rarely manage.
articles.whyItMatters: "Complete neighbourhood" is usually a transport/retail idea. Folding ecological renaturing into it — funded at EU scale — genuinely shifts the term's meaning.

articles.title: Melbourne Design Week 2026: 400+ events mark the decade
articles.summary: Melbourne Design Week 2026 returns 14–24 May for its tenth anniversary edition with more than 400 events spanning architecture, interiors, product design and emerging cross-disciplinary practice — the largest program the festival has staged. The decade marker positions Melbourne's design culture as a confident export rather than a regional showcase, with international visitors expected across a slate that includes exhibitions, symposia, studio openings, walking tours, and on-site previews of projects in construction. The Queen Victoria Market precinct emerges as a recurring centre of gravity. Cox Architecture's recently announced scheme — more than 1,500 dwellings opposite the soon-to-be-built Gurrowa Place development — sits at the western edge of the market and is one of several projects MDW is using to anchor conversation about the future of central Melbourne's residential mix. Several program threads engage directly with QV Market's redevelopment, treating it as a live brief rather than a finished case study, with curated walks and roundtables built around the precinct's evolving public realm. Other strands cover circularity in product design, Indigenous-led design practice, and the emerging cohort of practice models being incubated through RMIT, Monash and Melbourne University design schools. The decade-long arc of MDW also lets the festival reflect on its own format — a number of sessions will examine whether the city-as-festival model still serves a profession increasingly distributed across hybrid and remote work. Full program drops late April; bookings open shortly after.
articles.whyItMatters: QV Market is now the de facto centre of gravity for Melbourne's next decade of urban thinking. Watch what gets programmed there over the festival's ten days.

articles.title: Melbourne group resurrects dormant Urban Design Forum
articles.summary: A group of Melbourne practitioners has relaunched the Urban Design Forum (UDF), reviving a more than 30-year-old organisation that had gone dormant during the pandemic. The reboot — formally announced at MPavilion — aims to galvanise like-minded peers around the major challenges facing Australian cities, with a stated mission to support "the design and governance of the city in the public interest" with a renewed sense of ambition. The relaunch fills a recognisable gap in the Melbourne discourse. Australian urban design currently runs through professional peak bodies (AILA, AIA, PIA), academic conferences and individual practice events, but lacks a forum dedicated specifically to urban design — distinct from architecture or planning — at the metropolitan scale. The UDF revival positions itself as that connective tissue: a space for cross-disciplinary debate among urban designers, landscape architects, planners, transport engineers and city-makers from local government. Programming will include public forums, members-only roundtables, research fellowships and an advocacy stream targeting state policy. Early sessions are framed around metropolitan-scale questions — the next refresh of Plan Melbourne, the role of public land in housing supply, density done well, and the governance gaps in growth-area councils — that the founders argue have lacked an organised professional voice. The committee includes practitioners drawn from large multidisciplinary firms, sole-practice studios, academia and local government, deliberately spanning generations. Subscriptions and event signups are now open through udf.org.au.
articles.whyItMatters: Professional forums shape what cities ask for. A revived UDF changes whose voices get into Plan Melbourne's next refresh — and how loud they are.

articles.title: University of Michigan launches one-year Master of Urban Technology
articles.summary: The University of Michigan's Taubman College of Architecture and Urban Planning will launch a one-year Master of Urban Technology degree — the first programme of its kind that can be completed in a single year at a US university. The new degree positions itself between traditional urban planning and computer science, targeting an emerging professional space defined by civic data, smart mobility, sensor networks, and neighborhood-scale digital infrastructure. The structure — accelerated, post-graduate, professionally oriented — signals a maturation of the urban-tech field, which until now has typically been pursued through graduate certificates inside planning faculties or specialised tracks within information schools. Taubman's programme commits a full year of dedicated coursework to the discipline, suggesting employer demand has reached the point where a standalone credential is viable. Course content will cover civic data infrastructure, urban analytics, mobility systems, public-sector digital procurement, and the ethics of automated decision-making in city services. Studio work pairs students with municipal partners on live projects — a model adapted from Taubman's existing planning studios. Graduates are positioned for hybrid roles in city governments, transit agencies, large engineering consultancies, and the emerging cohort of urban-tech vendors building everything from kerbside sensors to congestion-pricing systems. For practitioners watching this space, the launch is a useful signal: urban tech is consolidating from a sub-specialty into its own recognised discipline, and the credentialing infrastructure is starting to catch up. First intake opens in autumn.
articles.whyItMatters: Urban tech is consolidating from a sub-specialty into its own discipline. Watch the credentialing infrastructure — that's where the field's centre of gravity moves next.

articles.title: New reforms legislated for Victorian apartment buildings
articles.summary: Victoria's new Mid-Rise Code commenced operation on 16 April 2026, introducing deemed-to-comply planning standards for apartment buildings between four and six storeys in designated residential zones. Under the reform, developments that meet state-wide requirements are automatically compliant, with no standing for neighbours to appeal — a significant streamlining of an approval process long criticised as one of the slowest in the country. Clause 57 applies the rules to the Mixed Use Zone, the Township Zone inside Melbourne's urban growth boundary, the Residential Growth Zone, and the new Housing Choice and Transport Zone. The code abolishes the mandatory upper-level setback that produced the so-called "wedding cake" silhouette across inner Melbourne, freeing designers to pursue simpler massing with better thermal performance. It also introduces tree canopy targets of 10–20 per cent on site, alongside new standards for privacy, daylight and communal open space. Heritage and flood overlays continue to operate in parallel, so character precincts are not automatically opened up. Premier Jacinta Allan framed the code as a delivery mechanism for "more homes, faster, in the places people want to live," and the package is pitched to absorb a meaningful share of Melbourne's mid-rise pipeline, where the "missing middle" has historically been trapped between townhouse codes and tower controls. Industry bodies have broadly welcomed the certainty while warning that apartment quality will hinge on how rigorously the landscape and daylight standards are enforced under deemed-to-comply pathways.
articles.whyItMatters: For urban designers, the Mid-Rise Code reshapes the rulebook for Melbourne's mid-scale streets — trading neighbour-led objection rights for prescribed tree canopy, setback and daylight standards, and putting the onus on designers to make the deemed-to-comply envelope produce genuinely liveable buildings.

articles.title: 'Grocery-to-housing' developer proposes 415-unit senior tower pair at Oakland Trader Joe's site
articles.summary: San Francisco developer Align has filed an application to replace the Trader Joe's–anchored retail centre at 5727 College Avenue in Oakland's Rockridge neighbourhood with a pair of senior-housing high-rises of 31 and 25 storeys, delivering 415 residences on a 1.5-acre parcel beside the Rockridge BART station. The programme is split between 371 independent-living units, 18 assisted-living units and 26 memory-care units, with an undisclosed non-profit operator lined up to run the facility. Align filed under California's density bonus law and a statute that exempts qualifying housing projects from CEQA environmental review, allowing the scheme to move forward at heights well above the site's base zoning. The firm has previously pursued a similar "grocery-to-housing" model over several Safeway stores in San Francisco, where the grocery component is typically rebuilt at the base of the new towers. Rockridge is different: Albertsons, Safeway's parent, owns the site but is not proposing to replace the Trader Joe's, which is the neighbourhood's only full-service grocer and also the first unionised Trader Joe's in the United States. Community response has been sharply split between housing advocates welcoming dense, transit-adjacent senior homes and residents worried about a food-access desert, the loss of a unionised workforce, and the scale of 31-storey towers dropped into a low-rise commercial strip.
articles.whyItMatters: The proposal is a stress test for transit-oriented infill under California's density bonus and CEQA exemptions — showing how aggressively those tools can override local scale and land-use expectations, and how "grocery-to-housing" conversions land very differently when the grocery doesn't come back.

articles.title: Portland, Maine approved a record number of homes last year — but far fewer got built
articles.summary: Portland, Maine's planning board approved 1,420 new housing units in 2025 — a city record and nearly a 200 per cent jump from the 477 units approved the year before — yet only 187 homes were actually added to the city's stock, a 68 per cent drop in completions from 2024. The approval surge was concentrated: a handful of particularly large proposals accounted for roughly 95 per cent of the approved units, meaning most of the pipeline rides on the viability of a few deals rather than a broad base of small and mid-sized projects. Developers have repeatedly cited construction costs, interest rates, and the city's 11-year-old inclusionary zoning rule — which requires 25 per cent of units in new buildings to be deemed affordable, or a per-unit opt-out fee — as the decisive barrier between an approved drawing and a finished building. Portland officials launched a formal review of the IZ policy in December, with findings expected in the coming weeks. Housing advocates are split: some argue the approval record proves the policy still attracts development and should be kept; others, including builders, say the gap between 1,420 approvals and 187 completions is itself the evidence that the numbers no longer pencil. The debate previews a wider reckoning for mid-size US cities balancing affordability mandates against feasibility.
articles.whyItMatters: The 1,420-versus-187 gap reframes how urban designers should read "housing wins" — permitting data alone masks whether approved projects are actually deliverable, and whether affordability instruments are cross-subsidising homes or quietly stalling them.
